q: How are client script files stored and deployed in a model-driven app?
why: Client scripts are uploaded as JavaScript web resources referenced by forms.

q: Your script calls functions from a shared helper library. What must you configure so it loads?
o: A web resource dependency on the library
why: Declaring web resource dependencies ensures dependent libraries load before your script runs.

q: Decide whether each client-scripting statement is true.

rows.s: Event handlers can be registered from a form's properties.
Event handlers can also be registered in code (addOnChange, etc.).
Client script is authored in C#.

why: Handlers register via form properties or code (addOnChange/addOnSave); client script is JavaScript.

q: Which form events commonly trigger client scripts?
why: OnLoad, OnSave and OnChange are the core form/field events for client scripts.

q: What does the execution context passed to a handler primarily give you?
o: Access to the form context (data and UI) for the current record
why: The execution context exposes getFormContext() to reach the form's data and UI.

q: Which object do you use to read/write a column's value on the form?
why: getAttribute returns the attribute whose getValue/setValue read and write the column.

q: Which retrieves a control (not the data) to change UI, e.g. hide it?
why: getControl returns the control for UI operations like setVisible/setDisabled.

q: Decide whether each context statement is true.

rows.s: Passing the execution context to functions is the recommended pattern.
You can navigate tabs and sections through the form context.

why: Pass the execution context; use getControl (not getAttribute) for visibility; tabs/sections are reachable via the form context.

q: To hide a form SECTION when a field is empty, which approach is correct?
o: getControl on the section (via tab.sections) and call setVisible(false)
Delete the section from the table
why: Client script toggles section visibility with setVisible via the tab/section objects.

q: Which is the recommended way to make a field required from client script?
why: setRequiredLevel controls requirement level at runtime from client script.

q: Decide whether each common-task statement is true.

rows.s: You can show/hide tabs as well as sections.
addOnChange registers a handler for a field's change event.

why: Tabs and sections both support visibility; addOnChange wires a field's OnChange.

q: The exercise prepares which artifact first so the script can be added to a form?
why: You prepare a solution with the form so the web resource and handlers can be added.

q: Why pass the execution context as the first parameter to your handler function?
o: So the function can call getFormContext() without relying on globals
why: Passing the context lets the function obtain the form context in a supported, reliable way.

q: A script runs on OnLoad but should also react when a user edits a field. What do you add?
o: An OnChange handler for that field
why: Field edits fire OnChange; register a handler there in addition to OnLoad.

q: Decide whether each statement is true.

rows.s: Web resources can include libraries your main script depends on.
Handlers may be registered both declaratively and in code.
Client script always runs on the server.

why: Web resources host libraries; handlers register declaratively or in code; client script runs on the client.

q: A field's value must drive whether another control is enabled. Which pair do you use?
o: getAttribute to read the value, getControl to setDisabled
getControl to read the value, getAttribute to setVisible
why: Read data with getAttribute; change UI (enable/disable) with getControl.setDisabled.
